figures.caption: Figure 2: Seismic hazard curve for a specific site, based on data from the U.S. Geological Survey (USGS) [67].
Figure 5: Dynamic Bayesian Network (DBN) for the state-dependent generalized fragility model.

Figure 14: Comparison of total life-cycle costs, including maintenance/repair (M/R) costs and risk costs, for the optimal MDP policy and three different top-performing condition-based maintenance (CBM) policies.

Figure 6: Computational complexity comparison for a homogeneous system with |S(k)| = 16 states and |A(k)| = 4 actions per component, and c = 5 average non-zeros per row per component matrix. (a) Total floating-point operations per value iteration update. (b) Total memory requirement in GB. Vertical dashed line indicate the ratio between the naive and tensor-based methods at selected values of Nc.
Figure 10: Sample life-cycle realizations showing maintenance actions, CDS, and SDS over 50 years under the optimal MDP policy.

Figure 1: Conceptual illustration of risk evolution under three hazard analysis and maintenance strategies: (a) Static Risk Assessment, illustrating conventional PEER PBEE methodology, where discrete evaluations (red dots) assume the system remains at a constant pristine baseline risk, disregarding continuous background accumulation of deterioration and shock damage; (b) Reactive Maintenance, where interventions are triggered only when risk exceeds an acceptable threshold due to progressive deterioration and sudden shocks; and (c) the proposed Stochastic Optimal Control framework, which explicitly accounts for time-dependent deterioration and derives state-dependent, preventive interventions to optimally manage risk over the life-cycle.

Figure 3: Simulated trajectories of the nonstationary gamma process for section loss due to corrosion. The solid red line indicates the mean deterioration path, E[D(τ)], while the gray lines represent individual stochastic realizations.

Figure 11: Variation of probability of failure over time for all nodes. The red curves represent the case with no maintenance actions, while the blue curves show the outcomes under the MDP optimal policy.
Table 1: Definition of variables for the Park-Ang damage index (DPA).
Figure 12: Sample trajectories of system-level risk evolution over 50 years under the optimal policy.
Table 2: Notation for the MDP formulation.
Figure 7: Location and dependency of three infrastructure nodes. (a) The geographical location of nodes A, B, and C in San Francisco, CA. (b) The corresponding system dependency diagram.

Figure 13: Annual exceedance probability (AEP) vs. economic loss for different management policies. The static PEER PBEE baseline (dashed gray) assumes a pristine, time-invariant system and is the same as the No action policy at t=1. As the system ages (solid curves), the risk under the No action policy (orange) increases from t=25 to t=50. In contrast, the adaptive MDP policy (blue) consistently maintains a lower AEP at both time points, showing effective risk reduction through maintenance.
Figure 4: Flowchart of the structural simulation workflow for comprehensive performance assessment.

Figure 8: IM-conditional state transition probability matrix showing the probability of transitioning to a seismic damage state SDSj (columns) from an initial state SDSi (rows), conditioned on the corrosion damage state (CDS = 1, 2, 3) and IM . Each matrix of plots corresponds to a different level of pre-existing corrosion.

Figure 9: State-dependent fragility plots for a component with initial corrosion damage state CDS = 1. Each plot shows the probability of transitioning to a final seismic damage state SDSj conditioned on the initial state SDSi.
